text: Another natural-language feature in PLs (in fact the one that made me read that paper) is the use of names as references to the context of a program's application domain that are superfluous for machine execution but often essential for human readers. I suspect that this is the main reason why point-free programming hasn't made it into practice.

text: Is anyone aware of other work on the relation of PLs to natural languages?

text:
As I read the paper, a couple of thoughts crossed my mind:
• AFAICT, no definition of "theoretical language" or "concrete language" is given.
• To my understanding, it is incorrect to say that C enabled the creation of UNIX. UNIX was written in assembler. Later, UNIX V4 was written in K&R C (which is not what is called C today). According to Claude, UNIX wasn't even the first O/S written in a high level language. Multics was written in PL/I (but became too unwieldy). Multics' predecessor was written in assembler.

elements.text: As I read the paper, a couple of thoughts crossed my mind:
AFAICT, no definition of "theoretical language" or "concrete language" is given.

To my understanding, it is incorrect to say that C enabled the creation of UNIX. UNIX was written in assembler. Later, UNIX V4 was written in K&R C (which is not what is called C today). According to Claude, UNIX wasn't even the first O/S written in a high level language. Multics was written in PL/I (but became too unwieldy). Multics' predecessor was written in assembler.

text:
There's no precise definition, and I doubt one could neatly distinguish between the two, but the idea is
• Theoretical language: formal language for use in theorems and proofs. Example: lambda calculus.
• Concrete language: formal language used for writing programs to be run on a computer.
It's easy to find hybrids and counterexamples (APL comes to mind), but overall the distinction looks useful to me.

The path from K&R C to ANSI C is an example used in the paper. As for Unix, Wikipedia agrees with Claude: it was written in PDP-7 assembler initially.

elements.text: There's no precise definition, and I doubt one could neatly distinguish between the two, but the idea is
Theoretical language: formal language for use in theorems and proofs. Example: lambda calculus.
Concrete language: formal language used for writing programs to be run on a computer.

It's easy to find hybrids and counterexamples (APL comes to mind), but overall the distinction looks useful to me.

The path from K&R C to ANSI C is an example used in the paper. As for Unix, Wikipedia agrees with Claude: it was written in PDP-7 assembler initially.

text:
This description leaves me unsatisfied. The implication is that Sketchpad, UNIX V1, TurboPascal V1, Forth, Lisp 1.5, etc., were programmed in 'informal languages'. Is that a 3rd category?

Yet, IMO, assembler is "more formal" than machine code. And machine code is "more formal" than a breadboard filled with zillions of transistors and a spaghetti network of wires.

If there is a 3rd category, what is the difference between "informal languages" and "concrete languages"?

There seems to be an underlying implication that "concrete languages" are "better" than the kind of languages used for Sketchpad, UNIX V1, TurboPascal 1, etc. How? Was Sutherland being ad hoc or was he being "formal" in a way that is no longer considered "formal" by modern standards?

A technique that was used in the transition from transistors to machine code was to lasso functionality into small chunks and to create something akin to strands of DNA in the form of machine code subroutines that can be walked and interpreted by little barnacle processors (akin to RNA?). Could we vault to expressing new levels of complexity by re-applying this technique?

text: [btw, IMO, K&R C and ANSI C are 2 completely different languages. The former accentuates building systems software for a PDP-11 by generating assembler (albeit using only PVRsubroutines (parameterized, value-returning subroutines)) and the latter emphasizes solving type-consistency riddles]
